question_statement: A teacher believes that game-based learning can only be implemented using digital tools. What is the issue with this understanding?
hints: Participation can still happen in digital settings; the main issue is misunderstanding the flexibility of GBTLA.
GBTLA includes powerful offline approaches such as board, card, and paper-based games.
Workload is not the central issue here.
Assessment can still be conducted in multiple ways.

question_statement: Which classroom activity BEST represents offline game-based learning?
options: Students watch a video and take notes
Students solve problems using an online quiz
Students use cards to match terms with definitions
Teacher explains concepts on the board
hints: This is passive learning, not game-based.
This is digital, not offline.
Card matching is a strong offline game that promotes active learning.
This is teacher-centered instruction.

question_statement: Why are offline games effective in learning?
options: They promote interaction, discussion, and problem-solving
hints: The goal is not to save time but to improve learning quality.
Games support teaching, they do not replace it.
Assessment is separate from the main purpose of games.
Interaction and problem-solving are key to active learning.

question_statement: A teacher uses a board game in class but students only move pieces without discussing answers. What should the teacher do?
hints: The activity should be improved, not stopped.
Correct answers alone do not ensure understanding.
Discussion helps students explain reasoning and deepen learning.
Without discussion, learning remains superficial.

question_statement: Which of the following is an example of a paper-based game?
hints: This is a card-based activity.
Bingo is a classic paper-based learning game.
This is a board game.
This is an activity, not a structured paper-based game.

question_statement: A teacher wants students to understand a process (e.g., water cycle). Which offline game is most suitable?
hints: Sequencing helps students understand steps in order.
Bingo focuses on recall, not processes.
Matching focuses on relationships, not sequence.
This is better for review than conceptual understanding.

question_statement: Which of the following are types of offline classroom games? (Select all that apply)
hints: Board games support structured learning and movement.
Card games support matching and sequencing.
Paper-based games include bingo and sorting tasks.
This is digital, not offline.
This is not game-based learning.

question_statement: Which features describe effective use of board games in learning? (Select all that apply)
hints: Movement should be linked to learning tasks.
Collaboration strengthens understanding.
Random movement removes learning value.
Activities must connect to lesson concepts.
Memorization alone is not active learning.

question_statement: Which activities reflect effective use of card and paper-based games? (Select all that apply)
hints: Matching builds conceptual connections.
Sequencing supports understanding of processes.
This is passive learning.
Sorting develops analytical thinking.
Copying notes is not game-based learning.

question_statement: A teacher wants to implement offline games effectively. Which actions are appropriate? (Select all that apply)
options: Align the game with learning outcomes
Focus only on winning the game
Use games as integrated part of lesson plan
Use games to reinforce curriculum concepts
hints: Alignment ensures meaningful learning.
The goal is learning, not winning.
Discussion deepens understanding.
Games should be part of the lesson, not separate.
Games reinforce and strengthen concepts.
